questions.question: After enabling Rouge, your code blocks render with the right `<span class="k">` markup but no colours appear. What's missing?
choices: Rouge needs to be added to the `plugins:` list in `_config.yml`
The fence tags are wrong - Rouge needs lowercase language names
A Rouge theme stylesheet - generate one with `rougify style <name> > _sass/_rouge.scss` and import it
GitHub Pages strips Rouge output unless `safe: false` is set
questions.explanation: Rouge produces classed spans, not colours. The colour CSS is generated separately via the `rougify` CLI and imported into your stylesheet. Without it the markup is correct but unstyled.

questions.question: You want a 14-line code snippet rendered with line numbers and lines 5-7 highlighted. Markdown fenced blocks don't support this. What's the right tool?
choices: Switch the site from Rouge to Pygments
Use Jekyll's `highlight` Liquid tag with `linenos` and `hl_lines` options
Add a JavaScript library like Prism.js to the page
Disable kramdown and use raw HTML `<pre>` tags
questions.explanation: The `highlight` Liquid tag accepts options the Markdown fence can't express - line numbers, line offsets, and `hl_lines` for marking specific lines. It still goes through Rouge, so the colour scheme stays consistent.

questions.question: Why is `pre.innerText` (not `pre.innerHTML`) the right source for a copy-to-clipboard button on Rouge-highlighted code?
choices: `innerText` preserves the original source; `innerHTML` would paste the span markup Rouge added
`innerHTML` triggers a CSP violation on GitHub Pages
`innerText` is faster on large code blocks
questions.explanation: Rouge wraps every token in `<span>` tags. `innerText` returns the rendered plain text - what the reader actually sees and wants to paste. `innerHTML` would copy the wrapped HTML, which is useless to anyone pasting it into a terminal or editor.
